KAN Israel News, citing an Iranian-linked news network, reports that $3 billion was transferred from Abu Dhabi to Tehran aboard a private Boeing 737.

According to the report, Iran agreed to halt direct attacks on Israel after receiving a U.S. message, delivered through Qatar, that Israel would refrain from further strikes in Lebanon.

The funds were reportedly flown to Tehran’s Mehrabad Airport aboard UAE Royal Jet aircraft A6-RJF, which was tracked traveling from Abu Dhabi to Iran.

At this stage, the flight itself was  genuine (it happened during Irans airspace closure) and the presence of a UAE delegation in Tehran has been reported by multiple sources. 

There is no independent confirmation that $3 billion was transferred or that such an arrangement was reached.

The $3B claim should be treated as unverified.

At the start of the year, the consensus on Wall Street was close to unanimous. The dollar would weaken through 2026. Goldman Sachs put the dollar index in the low-90s, JPMorgan and MUFG landed in the same zone, and every one of those calls leaned on the same assumption: the Federal Reserve would cut rates and narrow the yield advantage that had supported the currency. The dollar declined about 11 percent in the first half of 2025, its steepest such drop since 1973, which made the bearish case look obvious. Then the war changed the inputs. The Hormuz oil shock pushed US headline inflation to 3.8 percent, the Fed lost its room to cut, and the dollar held near 99 instead of collapsing to the 90s. The most conssensual macro trade of the year became the one quietly running everybody over.

Don't get me wrong, the bearish case was not foolish. The dollar had just posted its worst first half in five decades, US growth was slowing, and a Fed easing cycle looked set to erode the rate gap that draws global capital into dollar assets. Strategists extrapolated that into a steady decline toward the low-90s and positioned for it. By the spring, the dollar was supposed to be well on its way down. Instead, the war rewrote the one variable the whole thesis depended on, which was the path of US interest rates.

The Hormuz premium did the damage to the forecast. Energy fed straight into US prices; headline CPI hit 3.8 percent in April, the highest reading since May 2023, and core PCE held at 3.3 percent. A central bank cannot ease into an inflation print that is reaccelerating on oil, so the cuts the dollar bears had penciled in for 2026 were postponed and then priced out entirely. The yield advantage that supports the dollar stayed wide. The currency held its ground while the consensus that bet against it slowly bled.

𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗱, 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗴
The distinction that matters for the next move is what is actually holding the dollar up. This is not raw strength. The DXY sits below 100, which is weak against its own history, and per RBC, the currency is still roughly 15 percent ovvervalued against major peers on a purchasing-power basis. The dollar is being held in place by a single external force: an inflation rate that will not let the Fed cut. Remove that force, and the floor the bears were waiting for opens up.

I keep one rule about trades the whole Street agrees on. When every bank is sure the dollar drops, I want to know exactly what they are all leaning on, because the assumption everyone shares is the one nobody is hedging. This time, they were leaning on Fed cuts, and a war in the Gulf kicked the leg out.

That reframes what a dollar position actually is right now. A short dollar bet is a bet that the Fed cuts soon, which is a bet that inflation rolls over, which is a bet that the oil premium fades, which is a bet that the Hormuz war ends. Stripped down, the crowded dollar short is a leveraged wager on peace. Yesterday's session, where the war flared and the blockade stayed in place, is the kind of event that keeps that wager underwater. The dollar bears need a signed deal, and Washington spent the weekend making one harder to reach.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗾𝘂𝗲𝗲𝘇𝗲 𝗜𝘁 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝘀
A dollar that holds while oil sits near $96 is a tightening delivered to the rest of the world, whether they want it or not. Comodities are priced in dollars, so an importer pays twice during this war, once for the higher barrel and again for the firmer currency it must buy to settle the bill. The countries with dollar-denominated debt face a rising real burden on every coupon. Capital that might have rotated into emerging markets stays home for the yield. The pressure shows up as weaker local currencies, tighter financial conditions, and imported inflation across the importing bloc.

Energy importers have oil priced in dollars + a firm dollar = double cost. India imports 85% of its crude. Japan's yen near 158 makes the barrel brutal. Europe pays up on both.
Dollar debtors: EM sovereigns and corporates with dollar liabilities face a heavier real burden as the currency holds and local FX slips. Refinancing gets dearer.
EM capital flows: a wide US yield gap keeps capital in dollars. EM equity and local debt see outflows, central banks defend currencies, growth slows.

Now I will state the structural counterweight plainly.
The dollar is firm in the spot market and questioned in the reserve system at the same time. Underneath the cyclical bid, central banks keep trimming Treasury holdings and adding gold, a diversification that the asset freezes of Russia and now Iran have accelerated. The honest limit on the bullish-dollar view is that today's firmness and tomorrow's erosion are both real, on different clocks: the war props the spot price now, while the weaponization of reserves chips at the dollar's monetary role over years. A trader can be long the dollar this quarter and still believe its reserve share declines this decade.

The catalyst that ends the pain trade is specific, and it is not a chart pattern. A genuine Hormuz settlement lets the oil premium deflate, hands the Fed room to cut, and finally delivers the dollar decline the Street has been waiting for since January. The April ceasefire offered a preview when the dollar softened on the hope of de-escalation. Until that deal is signed, the inflation stays hot, the Fed stays pinned into Warsh's June 16-17 meeting, and the dollar keeps grinding the shorts. The position that pays is the patient one that stops fighting the prop.

The entire Street bet on a weak dollar this year, and the war quietly turned that bet into a wager on peace by trapping the Fed with its own inflation. The shorts need a signed deal to be right, and Washington keeps the blockade on and the deal out of reach. So with Warsh eight days away and no cut in sight, how much longer can the most crowded trade on the screen keep paying the wrong side?

Daily Market Report #760

It’s Tuesday. The week started positive, and I am excited to get into the newest updates as always.

It is 2 days until the World Cup, and part of me is ready to let football take over the timeline for a few weeks.

Because the bounce I cautiously wrote about yesterday already has a war pressing on it.

We saw what happened in the Middle East again.

Let’s hope and pray for peace.

Korea had a Black Monday, with the KOSPI halted after falling 10%.

Then the Nasdaq dropped 4%.

Apple also had its yearly conference yesterday, where they showed Siri and AI updates.

And yet, crypto is somehow green this morning.

S&P futures even erased the losses and turned positive.

The disconnect between the chaos and the bounce is really crazy.

Fear & Greed is at 10.

Still pinned in extreme fear.

Bouncing inside maximum fear, to be honest.

Oil rose 5%, back toward the highs and almost touching $100.

Gold is sitting at $4,300.

It is actually down, which is unusual on a war-escalation day and tells you that the de-risking last week was so violent that even gold got sold for liquidity.

Silver is sitting at $73.

The macro this week is all about CPI tomorrow, PPI on Thursday, and jobless claims on Thursday.

After the hot prints that started this whole correction, these numbers decide if the bounce lives or dies.

Bank of America just warned that 70% of its bear-market indicators are now flashing.

Bitcoin is sitting at $63,236, up 0.7% on the day.

The big structural read this morning is brutal and important.

Strategy bought 1,550 BTC, roughly $100M, after selling 32 BTC.

It was a hard move for Michael Saylor to sell, but buying cheaper here answers the capitulation narrative directly.

Glassnode puts the BTC bottom zone between $46K and $54K.

JPMorgan says Strategy needs to increase cash reserves, but the buying directly answers the forced-seller narrative.

They are adding into the worst tape of the cycle.

Ethereum is sitting at $1,686.

ETH dominance is at 9%.

ETH/BTC at 2016 levels is the stat that should worry every ETH holder.

ETH co-founder Joe Lubin had to prop up an ETH margin loan, which is not exactly a good confidence signal.

This is the most hated Ethereum has been in years.

Historically, that kind of sentiment can mark a turn in the market.

But the chart has to prove it first.

Solana $67.01, up 2.4%.

Helius CEO signaled a Solana-exclusive privacy protocol as the next infrastructure priority, which is interesting given everything happening in the privacy sector right now.

The privacy-everywhere thesis keeps spreading to every L1.

XRP $1.17, up 2.1%. BNB $602, up 0.9%. Binance delisting last cycle runners. 

Hyperliquid $61.95, up 2%.

Recovering from the Hayes crash.

Coinbase is now the official deployer of Hyperliquid’s USDC treasury wallet, a real institutional tie-in.

Citrini Research called HYPE a compelling investment, noting that Hyperliquid accounted for nearly half of all crypto token buybacks in 2025.

Hayes still denies buying back in after dumping.

Monero $322, up 5.7%.

Zcash $450, up 5.2%, continuing the recovery from the silent-minting crash.

But here is the unsettling follow-up:

The researcher who found the ZEC Orchard bug is now looking at Monero.

After an Opus 4.8 audit cracked Zcash, the same scrutiny is turning to XMR.

If AI-assisted auditing finds something in Monero, the whole privacy sector gets retested.

Zcash proposed its Ironwood upgrade with formal verification and a supply-audit turnstile, exactly the right response to a counterfeiting scare.

Sui opened confidential transfers for public testing on Devnet.

Umbra launched private bridging across 32 chains.

Privacy is bruised but building faster than ever.

Bittensor $216, up 3.2%. Polkadot $0.97, up 1.2%. Arbitrum $0.0817, flat. Starknet $0.0341, up 1.4%. Pengu $0.0068, up 1.3%, Borg $0.1559, down 1.3%.

The Humanity Protocol hacker minted 100M H tokens worth $11.4M on BSC, and the token collapsed.

That is what happens when an exploit creates unlimited supply.

The NFT whitehat story got even better.

Yuga Labs recovered 65+ NFTs from the Flooring Protocol exploit, including 29 Bored Apes.

Meanwhile, Hyperliquid trader Arthur Huang got liquidated 10 times in 8 hours during the weekend volatility.

SpaceX’s $75B IPO is coming this week and is already 2x oversubscribed.

Kalshi shows Elon Musk’s trillionaire odds at a 97% all-time high.

OpenAI filed a confidential S-1 for its own IPO and is building ChatGPT into a superapp.

Altman is pushing for a US equity-stake backstop, and Trump is meeting AI execs on equity stakes this week.

Sam Altman laid out OpenAI’s 3 goals:

An automated AI researcher.

Accelerating the global economy.

Giving everyone a personal AGI.

The ambition is staggering, and the capital keeps flowing while crypto bleeds.

We built the rails, and AI took the money.

Sam Bankman-Fried formally applied for a presidential pardon from Trump. Can he buy his way out?

Mid-Day Markets - June 9, 2026

This Morning's Top Story

The macro narrative is holding a highly volatile, bifurcated posture today, June 9, 2026, as capital desks frantically adjust risk-management boundaries to match an escalating biosecurity crisis. 

The broad commodity tape is firmly under the influence of the "War on Screwworm" campaign, formally activated following a joint federal and state emergency summit. With Texas issuing a statewide disaster proclamation and Canada clamping down with an immediate 21-day livestock ban on Lone Star shipments, the meat complex is enduring a massive structural realignment that completely overshadows standard physical demand metrics.

In sharp contrast, the row-crop complex is functioning under heavy macro crosscurrents. The initial shock of last week's multi-year price washouts has faded into a defensive, data-driven stabilization. Automated trading networks are working to reconcile a highly static domestic supply baseline with a volatile global energy backdrop, leaving grain futures in a tight, protective pattern while industrial buyers wait out early-summer weather cycles.

Market News (Performance Review & Rebalancing)

Early-session capital allocation is aggressively favoring livestock risk premiums while grains rely on minor commercial flashes to sustain nearby floors.

Screwworm Crisis Escalates: The biosecurity landscape grew significantly more complex following the USDA's confirmation of new cases across multiple counties, including a calf in La Salle County and a travel-related canine case extending the pest's footprint. With 20-kilometer infested zones under rigid quarantine and aerial sterile fly dispersals beginning out of Edinburg, Texas, fears of a structural replacement cattle shortage are keeping a firm floor under livestock paper.

Grains Scrap Out Minor Inbound Gains: The row-crop complex is finding localized stabilization today, supported by a fresh morning flash export sale of domestic corn to international destinations. July Corn is grinding 1 to 4 cents higher, trading near $4.20 per bushel. The recovery follows yesterday's USDA Crop Progress report, which kept national corn condition scores steady at a moderate 67% good-to-excellent, matching expectations but shifting the internal mix toward higher "excellent" weightings. July Soybeans are hovering quietly around $11.23, managing narrow consolidation as the national planting pace reaches 92% complete.

Livestock Consolidates Near Highs: The meat pits are showing incredible resilience following last week's massive short-covering surge. August Live Cattle are posting modest gains at midsession, hovering near $241.80, while August Feeders remain highly firm near $354.25. The screen continues to aggressively price out the potential loss of Mexican stocker imports and cross-border shipping channels, forcing packers to work around tight near-term procurement boundaries despite their ongoing efforts to leverage heavy, long-fed domestic inventories.

Institutional funds are running a highly targeted, two-speed model today. Systemic trading advisers have officially suspended their macro grain shorting models, choosing to let the 67% national corn rating absorb nearby export volume. However, the true speculative urgency remains locked inside the livestock complex. 

Capital allocation desks are prioritizing physical asset protection, treating the disaster declarations across the Southern Plains as a structural mandate to reduce short paper exposure and accumulate high-integrity meat contracts.
